source.title: Droplet-based microfluidic platform for high-throughput screening of Streptomyces

initial_request.research_problem: Traditional Streptomyces screening methods (microtiter plates, flow cytometry) are low-throughput or cannot screen mycelium and secreted products, limiting industrial strain engineering.
initial_request.known_context: Flow cytometry-based screening can only analyze spores or protoplasts, not filamentous mycelium or secreted products, which are critical for industrial Streptomyces cultivation.
quantifiable_goals: Throughput at least 10,000 variants per hour

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Flow cytometry-based screening can only analyze spores or protoplasts, not filamentous mycelium or secreted products.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No high-throughput method exists to screen Streptomyces in its mycelial form or to screen secreted products.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A droplet-based microfluidic platform can enable high-throughput screening of Streptomyces mycelium and secreted products, overcoming flow cytometry limitations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a droplet-based microfluidic platform for Streptomyces screening.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on known limitations of flow cytometry.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design spore preparation for droplet encapsulation.

evidence.quote_or_span: Previously reported flow cytometry-based screening methods can only screen spores or protoplasts released from mycelium, which do not represent the filamentous stationary phase Streptomyces used in industrial cultivation.
Here we show a droplet-based microfluidic platform to facilitate more relevant, reliable and rapid screening of Streptomyces mycelium

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need to encapsulate spores in droplets.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Spore clumping may hinder monodispersed encapsulation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using eight-layered sterile filter papers can obtain monodispersed spore suspension for droplet encapsulation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Optimize spore preparation using eight-layered sterile filter papers.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Eight-layered sterile filter papers performed the best for obtaining monodispersed spores.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test germination and growth in droplets.

evidence.quote_or_span: eight-layered sterile filter papers were found to perform the best

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Monodispersed spores obtained; need to verify droplet compatibility.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown if spores can germinate and grow normally in droplets.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Spores will germinate and grow comparably in droplets as in suspension.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare germination and growth in droplets vs. suspension.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Spores germinated and proliferated at a comparable rate; droplets filled with mycelium by 24 h.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Determine fluorescence detection timeline for screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: spores germinate and proliferate at a comparable rate in suspension and droplet conditions
At 24 h, the droplets were almost filled with mycelium

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Growth confirmed; need to establish fluorescence detection window.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Optimal time for fluorescence-based screening unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: eGFP fluorescence will become detectable and peak within a defined window.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Monitor eGFP fluorescence over time in droplets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: eGFP fluorescence detectable at 12 h, peaked at 24 h, intense until day 3, detectable until day 7.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate FADS enrichment using eGFP model.

evidence.quote_or_span: After 12 h of cultivation, the signals became detectable by fluorescence microscopy and continued to intensify until 24 h. The signals remained intense until day 3 and weakened thereafter, but remained detectable until day 7

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Fluorescence window defined; need to validate sorting accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: FADS can enrich eGFP-positive strains from a mixed library with high enrichment ratio.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform FADS enrichment of eGFP-positive strain from mixed library.

evidence.quote_or_span: After comprehensive optimization, our platform achieved a screening throughput of at least 10,000 variants per hour with an enrichment ratio $^{24}$ of up to 334.2.
After comprehensive optimization, our platform achieved a screening throughput of at least 10,000 variants per hour with an enrichment ratio $^{24}$ of up to 334.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: FADS validated; need to quantify promoter strengths.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Relative strengths of constitutive promoters in droplets unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: FADS-measured promoter strengths will correlate with plate-based assays.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure promoter strengths by FADS and compare with plate assays.
evolution_trajectory.observation: rpsL(XC) strongest; gapdh(EL)p 69.0% of that; results consistent with plate assays.

evidence.quote_or_span: The FADS analysis indicated that rpsL (XC) was the strongest promoter and that the strength of gapdh (EL)p was 69.0% of that of rpsL(XC).
The FADS analysis indicated that rpsL (XC) was the strongest promoter and that the strength of gapdh (EL)p was 69.0% of that of rpsL(XC).
These results were consistent with those from 24-well plate cultivation, indicating the good reliability and accuracy of the FADS-assisted analysis of Streptomyces.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Promoter characterization complete; need to screen libraries for desired strengths.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Variants with desired promoter strengths not isolated.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: FADS can isolate promoter variants with desired strengths from synthetic libraries.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen ermE* and gapdh promoter libraries by FADS, selecting top 1% droplets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Variants isolated; reconstructed strains validated in plates; results similar to plate cultivation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test cellulase production in droplets with different carbon sources.

evidence.quote_or_span: vectors containing each promoter variant were reconstructed and transformed into wild-type S. lividans 66, and the resulting re-constructed strains were subjected to FADS analysis.
these results were similar to those from 24-well plate cultivation
Three gapdh(EL)p variants, g\_C1p, g\_C4p, and g\_D1p, were identified, which exhibited 10.1%, 24.
As a proof-of-concept, $\sim30,000$ and 6000 colonies of e-lib and g-lib, respectively, which harbored different promoter variants, were collected and subjected to high-throughput screening via FADS.
Therefore, we first aimed to screen out the variants that were stronger than the original $ermE^{*}p$ , by selecting the top 1% of droplets with the strongest signals from e-lib.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Promoter libraries screened; need to test cellulase production in droplets.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Optimal carbon source for cellulase induction in droplets unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Lactose or xylan can induce cellulase production in droplets.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test cellulase production in droplets with lactose, xylan, and R2YE medium.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Lactose induced fluorescence at 24 h; xylan at 36 h; R2YE at 48 h; xylan caused droplet shrinkage after 48 h.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Xylan unsuitable; select lactose for further screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: The droplets containing xylan or lactose medium began to exhibit green fluorescence at 36 h and 24 h of cultivation, respectively
droplets containing R2YE liquid medium did not exhibit green fluorescence until 48 h
droplets began to shrink markedly after 48 h when xylan was used as the carbon source
such unstable droplets were not suitable for further screening

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Lactose selected as carbon source; need to screen mutant library for hyper-cellulase producers.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: FADS can isolate hyper-cellulase producers from ARTP mutant library.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen ARTP mutant library in lactose droplets by FADS, sorting top 5% fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Six hyper-cellulase producers identified with 69.2–111.4% enhanced activity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Sequence cellulase-coding genes to identify mutations.

evidence.quote_or_span: A random library was constructed by ARTP mutagenesis of wild-type S. lividans 66 spores.
the droplets were screened at 24 h, and those with top 5% fluorescence intensities were sorted
The six best mutants, which exhibited 69.2–111.4% enhanced cellulase production than the wild type, were selected
A random library was constructed by ARTP mutagenesis of wild-type S. lividans 66 spores.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hyper-cellulase producers identified; need to understand genetic basis.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Mutations responsible for enhanced cellulase production unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations in cellulase-coding genes may explain enhanced production.
evolution_trajectory.observation: No mutations found in cellulase-coding genes; other genomic changes likely responsible.

evidence.quote_or_span: The cellulase-coding genes of these six hyperproducers of cellulases were further amplified and sequenced but were not found to contain any mutations when compared to the wild-type gene sequence.
we speculated that mutations in other parts of the genome might have positively regulated and enhanced cellulase production and/or secretion.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Platform demonstrated for multiple applications; need to summarize advantages.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to confirm platform overcomes flow cytometry limitations.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: FADS enables screening of mycelial form, supports entire life cycle, and screens extracellular products.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate platform advantages over flow cytometry.
evolution_trajectory.observation: FADS screens mycelium, supports life cycle up to 7 days, screens extracellular products.

evidence.quote_or_span: The most important advantage of the FADS-assisted method over the flow cytometry-based high-throughput screening methods of Streptomyces is that it enables the screening of Streptomyces in its mycelial form
Our study showed that droplets can support the entire life cycle of Streptomyces up to 7 days, including germination, vegetative growth, secondary metabolism, and autocytolysis

the soft Streptomyces mycelium combined with the long-term stability of droplets (i.e., several months) means that the FADS-assisted method is suitable for engineering Streptomyces strains for use in longer fermentation.
Another advantage of the FADS-assisted method overflow cytometry-based high-throughput screening methods is its ability to screen extracellular products.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Platform validated; need to note limitations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Platform applicability limited to fluorogenic products and tested strain.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Current FADS method cannot screen non-fluorogenic metabolites and may need adaptation for other strains.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Most secondary metabolites not fluorogenic; only tested on S. lividans 66.

evidence.quote_or_span: Most secondary metabolites are not fluorogenic and therefore cannot be directly screened by the current FADS method.
vectors containing each promoter variant were reconstructed and transformed into wild-type S. lividans 66, and the resulting re-constructed strains were subjected to FADS analysis.

metrics: Throughput at least 10,000 variants per hour
success_verification.final_conclusion: FADS enables high-throughput screening of Streptomyces mycelium and secreted products, overcoming flow cytometry limitations, with demonstrated applications in promoter engineering and cellulase hyperproducer isolation.
limitations: Most secondary metabolites are not fluorogenic, limiting direct FADS screening.
Platform only tested on S. lividans 66; non-sporulating strains may require adaptation.
